title: Finding Regular Subgraphs
speaker: Richard Montgomery
abstract:
Finding regular subgraphs can be useful. Many results assume a graph is regular or are easier to prove when they are. In 1975, Erdős and Sauer asked for an estimate, for any constant r, on the maximum number of edges an n-vertex graph can have without containing an r-regular subgraph (one in which each vertex is in r edges). The best upper bound on this problem was for a long time one of Pyber from 1985, but the last few years have seen rapid developments, initiated by Janzer and Sudakov, and we now have an efficient framework to find regular subgraphs for not only constant r but the whole range of possible values of r. 
I will discuss this framework and its components, which include algebraic techniques of Alon, Friedland and Kalai, the recent breakthroughs on the sunflower conjecture, techniques to find almost-regular subgraphs developed from Pyber’s work, and, crucially, a novel random process that efficiently finds a very nearly regular subgraph in any almost-regular graph.
Joint work with Debsoumya Chakraborti, Oliver Janzer and Abhishek Methuku.

proof_stages.label: Extremal problem and Turán context
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the function asking how much average degree in an n-vertex graph forces a nonempty r-regular subgraph, motivates exact regularity, and positions the question among classical Turán-type forcing problems.

proof_stages.label: Known threshold landscape and new two-regime result
proof_stages.summary: After introducing the notation d_r(n), the speaker reviews the Alon-Friedland-Kalai, Pyber-Rödl-Szemerédi, and Janzer-Sudakov milestones and states the new bounds showing different behavior for constant/small r and larger r, with near-tightness except around a transition range.

proof_stages.label: Regularization pipeline and loss bookkeeping
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is organized as a passage from an arbitrary dense graph to an almost regular subgraph, then to a very nearly regular subgraph, and finally to an exactly r-regular subgraph, with the main average-degree loss occurring in the first step and the last step supplied by algebraic polynomial-method tools.

proof_stages.label: One-step near-regularization lemma
proof_stages.summary: The main new ingredient is formulated as an iterative lemma: from a graph whose degrees lie between d and roughly (1+gamma)d, one finds a nonempty subgraph with only slightly smaller average scale but a multiplicatively narrower degree interval.

proof_stages.label: Biased random thinning construction
proof_stages.summary: To prove the lemma, vertices are split into low- and high-degree sets, edges inside the low set are discarded harmlessly, and then low vertices and cross-edges are randomly deleted with probabilities chosen to penalize high-degree vertices more than low-degree ones.

proof_stages.label: Degree estimates, concentration, and iteration
proof_stages.summary: The expected surviving degrees are computed at the bottom and top of the interval, concentration is invoked to make the estimates simultaneous, and repeated application yields a very nearly regular subgraph while retaining useful randomness for further applications.
